text.title: stream-json-orchestration Worked Examples
text.summary: Five reference orchestrations: Discord live UI, budget cap, safety veto, conditional hand-off, OTel telemetry.

example.title: Discord bot live-updates from assistant events
example.flow: User runs `/agent fix the login bug` → bot spawns `claude -p ... --output-format stream-json` → each `assistant` event becomes a Discord message edit → `result` event closes with the final cost.
example.delta: Without stream-json, the user waits silently for 2 minutes; with it, the bot streams partial reasoning live.

example.title: $0.50 per-task budget cap
example.flow: Production agent service caps each task at $0.50. Orchestrator tracks `total_cost_usd` from each event; when cumulative spend exceeds the cap, `proc.kill()` fires even mid-tool-call.
example.delta: faion-net 2026-Q1 post-mortem: 12% of total spend was tasks that would have run unbounded; cap eliminated that class.

example.title: File-delete veto
example.delta: The veto fires before the tool actually runs because `tool_use` events arrive before tool execution. Saves restoring from backup.

example.title: Planner → executor hand-off
example.flow: Stage 1 planner emits a plan in its final assistant message → orchestrator parses the plan → spawns N parallel Stage 2 executor subprocesses (one per task) → each Stage 2 has its own stream → orchestrator gathers final results → Stage 3 reviewer reads aggregate. All connected via stream-json + Python `asyncio.gather`.
example.delta: No SDK, no shared in-process state; each subagent gets its own context-firewalled stream.

example.title: OTel GenAI semantic-conventions export
example.flow: Each event maps to an OpenTelemetry span: `system/init` → root span, `assistant` → child span `gen_ai.message`, `tool_use` → child span `gen_ai.tool.execution`, `result` → close root with cost and duration. Langfuse / Phoenix consumes the OTLP feed.
example.delta: Every agent run becomes a queryable trace; cost and duration are first-class span attributes.
